/hashstack.title: Case Study | Hashstack Redesign
/hashstack.description: Redesigned a complex DeFi platform to simplify borrowing, earning, and swapping for both web2 and web3 users.
p: Simplifying a DeFi lending protocol for real-world users
Hashstack is a permissionless DeFi lending protocol that enables users to borrow up to 500% of their collateral, offering significantly higher capital efficiency compared to traditional platforms.
It is designed for users who want to leverage their assets for trading or liquidity without relying on centralized approval systems.
The protocol was powerful, but difficult to navigate and understand.
This created friction in a product where clarity and trust are critical.
Most DeFi products are designed around protocol structure rather than user intent.
how the system works vs how users think

As a Product Designer, I collaborated closely with the stakeholders and engineers to define the user experience for tracking flow. During the platform migration from Starknet to Base, I led the end-to-end redesign of the product experience, working closely with the team to simplify complex DeFi interactions and make the platform more accessible to a broader audience.
The focus was not on changing the protocol, but on reducing the cognitive effort required to understand and use it.
With limited time during migration, I relied on real user signals:
These insights revealed consistent patterns in how users struggled with the product.
The design philosophy revolved around effortless simplicity. Every interaction was crafted to minimize friction and guide users naturally through the payment process.
Users struggled to understand the product
ProblemUsers frequently paused to interpret screens, searched for external explanations, and clicked without clear direction. → The interface required prior knowledge of DeFi.
DecisionDesign for clarity without assuming user familiarity.
WhyUsers should be able to understand and act without relying on external learning.
1. Simplified structure and clearer entry points.
ProblemCore actions were spread across multiple sections with inconsistent labeling → Users couldn’t build a mental model of the system.
WhyUsers think in goals (earn, borrow), not protocol components.
ProblemActions like repay, withdraw, and supply were spread across multiple screens. → Users had to jump between pages to complete a single task.
DecisionUnify core actions into a single interface.
WhyReducing navigation improves clarity and task efficiency.
Users lacked visibility into their positions
ProblemThere was no clear way to track borrowed assets, collateral, or performance. → Users couldn’t confidently manage their positions.
DecisionIntroduce a centralized view of user state.
WhyVisibility builds confidence, especially when users are dealing with real money.
1. Real-time tracking of borrow and earn positions.
ProblemTerms like “Collateral” and “Staked” lacked context. → New users struggled to interpret actions.
DecisionSimplify terminology and provide contextual guidance.
WhyUnderstanding should not depend on prior DeFi knowledge.
Visual density reduced clarity and trust
ProblemDense layouts and outdated UI made the interface harder to scan and trust.
DecisionIntroduce a cleaner, more structured visual system.
WhyClarity directly impacts trust in financial products.
Improved clarity in navigation and core actions
Reduced cognitive effort for new users
This project reinforced a key shift —not simplifying the protocol, but making its complexity more understandable.

DeFi systems are inherently complex, but users don’t need to see that complexity all at once.The focus became aligning the experience with user intent, while staying true to how the system works underneath.
In financial products, clarity directly impacts trust and decision-making.
Designing Hashstack reinforced a simple idea — the most effective products don’t remove complexity, they make it understandable.
This principle continues to shape how I approach product design.
We’ll nudge you when only 20% (₹600) is left to spend.
Your expense finally found his home.

/trackup.title: Case Study | Trackup
/trackup.description: Designed a smart expense tracking experience focused on clarity, habit-building, and effortless money management.
p: Making budgeting usable in real life
TrackyUp is a mobile-first budgeting experience designed for real-world usage — where attention is low, tracking is inconsistent, and financial decisions happen in the moment.
Instead of adding more features, the focus was on reducing the effort required to build a sustainable habit.
Budgeting doesn’t fail because people lack intent. It fails because the tools don’t align with real behavior.
1.  Require effort at the wrong time
As a result, users start with intent — but rarely sustain the habit.
People open budgeting apps when something goes wrong:
This creates a gap between how tools are designed and how they are actually used.

As a Freelancer, I collaborated closely with the client and engineers to define the user experience for tracking flow. I led the end-to-end design process, from discovery to final UI bringing in user-centered thinking, wireframes and high-fidelity prototypes. My role was to ensure that design aligned with business goals while maintaining a seamless and intuitive experience for users.
To understand this gap, I combined personal experience with lightweight validation:
The goal was to reduce friction at the moment of action and make budgeting a habit users could realistically sustain.
The design philosophy revolved around effortless simplicity. Every interaction was crafted to minimize friction and guide users naturally through the payment process.
ProblemExisting apps required too many steps to log a simple expense.
DecisionReduce expense logging to a single-step action.
WhyIn real-world usage, users prioritize speed over structure.
ProblemUsers dropped off before experiencing value due to upfront setup.
WhyReducing cognitive load early increases adoption and trust.
3. Contextual prompts instead of upfront forms.
ProblemUsers struggled to interpret dashboards and numbers quickly.
DecisionReplace data-heavy views with state-based grouping.
WhyUsers need quick understanding, not analysis.
ProblemMost apps notify users only after overspending.
WhyUsers need time to adjust behavior before crossing limits.
ProblemAutomated tools either created noise or required intrusive access.
WhyUsers want convenience without losing control or privacy.
3. User retains full control over entries.
ProblemManual entry for offline purchases increased effort.
WhyMaintains low effort across both online and offline transactions.
Tested with 6 users, focusing on speed and first-time experience.
- Faster task completion in quick-add flow
Designing TrackyUp reinforced a simple idea — the most effective products don’t add more features, they reduce the effort required to use them.
This principle continues to shape how I approach product design.

/.title: Sehaj Sharma — Product Designer
/.description: Turning complex ideas into seamless digital experiences across web and mobile. Explore the work and let’s connect.
p: so I make complex things simple.
of experience designing with people at the center.
I take complex workflow and break them down into experiences that are easy to understand, navigate, and use.
I focus on real user problems and design solutions that improve both the user experience and business outcomes.
I design with consistency and scale in mind, creating systems that grow with the product, not just individual screens.
I design products that make complex things easier for people.
not because it was the “safe” option, but because I’ve always been curious about how things work. I used to imagine being a hacker, breaking down systems, solving puzzles behind the screen.
But somewhere along the way, I found myself sketching more than coding.

Out of curiosity, I took up an internship with an illustration kit — no big plan, just a random decision that ended up shifting everything. It made me realise design wasn’t just about making things pretty — it was about making them make sense.

It made me realise — even the simplest illustrations or characters often carry deep stories. Behind every shape is structured thought, a reason, a decision. And since I already had a soft corner for drawing since childhood, I naturally started diving deeper into design — slowly learning that UX is where creativity meets intention.
